resources.string: Free work with readable odds, starter rewards, and no contract cost.
Riskier work with bigger clears, stronger unlock gates, and better payoff pressure.
%1$d stored. Spend 1 Special Contract on a quest clause for XXL gold and loot.
Repeat: %1$d base gold, XP, and loot rolls
One-time reward gained: %1$s. Recruit from Heroes when you get home.
45 s. Low risk. High chance of cave-related paperwork.
90 s. Medium risk. Bring a map, then someone who distrusts maps.
180 s. Medium risk. Bandits discovered forms and became everyone\'s problem.
180 s. Medium risk. The holy water is mostly brine and complaints.
180 s. Medium risk. The cargo is legal if nobody asks beautifully.
180 s. Medium risk. Hold the bread-crate barricade and count the biscuits.
240 s. High risk. Bring lockpicks, forms, and someone the door respects.
180 s. High risk. The dead demand interest and misfiled invoices.
210 s. Medium risk. The checkpoint inspector loves receipts more than blood.
240 s. High risk. A sanctioned caravan still collects penalties in triplicate.
270 s. High risk. Everyone wears a robe, a lantern, and a form in triplicate.
300 s. High risk. The official meal is compulsory; forgetting a form starts an emergency audit.
330 s. High risk. Every vow has witnesses, clauses, and emergency seating.
360 s. High risk. The booth sank years ago, but the fee schedule learned to swim.
390 s. High risk. The throne paid for something heroic and misplaced the proof.
420 s. High risk. The stairs go left, the roof goes down, and the lease is terrified.
Finished. The party returned with loot, mud, and a report nobody signed.
Complete %1$d/%2$d quests to license Rare loot in expedition rewards.
Rare loot licensed: expedition rewards now include a %1$d%% Rare item chance.
Upgrade the Notice Board for better quest rewards.
Reset heroes, items, guild perks, achievements, quest progress, and active runs. Gold, reputation, and guild level stay editable above.
%1$d/%2$d assigned. Passive guild income: %3$dg per hour.
%1$d s counted. Expedition crew rate %2$dg/h; idle rate %3$dg/h.
Small things happened while the guild was unsupervised.
Open the expedition report before planning the next disaster.
Sort the reward loot pile before launching another job.
Swap toward %1$s to raise odds from %2$d%% to %3$d%%.
Equip %1$s on %2$s before the next run.
Recruit another hero for %1$dg; the roster is still thin.
Claim ready achievements before spending. Paperwork is finally paying rent.
Earn %1$d more reputation to unlock the next mess.
Complete %1$d more quests to unlock the next mess.
Start %1$s; it is the best open payday right now.
No immediate equip upgrade. You can bank this for a later build.
The guild is becoming useful by accident. Facilities now change quest rewards, prep power, hero XP, and party size.
45 s. Low risk. High chance of cave-related paperwork.
Rare. They do not make you faster. They make leaving feel justified.
Brugg tried diplomacy. The door remained unconvinced.
Mira found a ring. It was already judging everyone.
The healer healed morale. Nobody noticed.
The guild is briefly calm. Pick a quest before the furniture gets ideas.
The party returns in %1$d s. Paperwork is happening to someone else.
The party returned. The report is stained, signed, and probably admissible.
No finished expedition is waiting here.
%1$d XP also fell out of the paperwork.
%1$d XP total (%2$d base, +%3$d from Training Yard +%4$d%%).
%1$d loot candidates will be revealed; the guild can only bring back a limited number.
Debug-only bonus: +%1$d gold and +%2$d extra loot rolls. Normal collect stays available.
The cave lost the argument and most of its contents.
The job was completed with only cosmetic panic.
Success arrived late, bruised, and carrying a receipt.
The expedition failed, but the lesson was aggressively documented.
Everyone survived. The map is requesting legal counsel.
The guild continued without you. This has made everyone dangerously confident.
%1$s came back while you were away.
Report ready: +%1$d completed, +%2$d claims, +%3$d seals pending.
Ticket inventory ready at the recruitment desk.
The report pushed ticket rewards closer to the desk.
The party had %1$d s to rehearse excuses after returning.
Check the roster, odds, and reward preview before sending everyone into the cave.
%1$d pity gold, up to %2$d loot items on a great success
Start a quest, survive the paperwork, then come back here with something shiny.
%1$d items banked. The quartermaster is pretending this is organized.
The unchosen loot is gone. Back to the guild.
%1$d/%2$d recovery slots used. Unchosen loot is destroyed when the pack is full.
This reward pile can recover %1$d item(s) before the rest is lost.
%1$d/%2$d selected. Browse the whole pile before confirming; everything else is lost.
Keep %1$d item(s) and destroy %2$d unselected item(s). The recovery crew only makes one trip.
Selling removes this item from inventory.
%1$s %2$s. Bonus +%3$d. %4$d items banked. Sell it for %5$d gold or equip it from Heroes.
%1$s %2$s. Bonus +%3$d. %4$d candidates in the pile; %5$d recovery choice(s) left. Unchosen loot will be destroyed.
+10% quest gold per extra level. Also fewer arrows through job postings.
Now +%1$d%% quest gold. Next: +%2$d%% and clearer quest gates.
Maxed at +%1$d%% quest gold. The board is dangerously legible.
+8 party power and +10%% hero XP per extra level. Mostly supervised shouting.
Now +%1$d party power and +%2$d%% hero XP. Next: +%3$d power and +%4$d%% XP.
Maxed at +%1$d party power and +%2$d%% hero XP. The shouting has structure now.
+1 quest party slot per extra level. The beds are very negotiable.
Now +%1$d slot over quest base. Next: +%2$d for messier parties.
Maxed at +%1$d slots over quest base. Nobody sleeps alone, possibly by law.
Reveals risky contract plan warnings and adds passive scouting reports.
Now %1$d warning(s). Next: %2$d warning(s) and clearer unlock previews.
Maxed at %1$d plan warning(s). The map has opinions now.
Adds low-frequency passive loot finds when Scout Table is active.
Maxed at %1$d%% passive loot-find chance. The racks are watching.
Turns downtime into supplies and raises the offline income cap.
Now +%1$d min offline cap. Next: +%2$d min.
Maximum +%1$d min offline cap. The stew has logistics.
Spend %1$d gold to hire one dramatically qualified applicant.
%1$s already has a guild badge. Contract converted into +%2$d Rep.
Achievement rewards can be spent here instead of gold.
A normal recruitment pull from the current catalog.
Pull from a narrower specialist class pool.
Guarantee: recruits only from this class pool (%1$s).
Recruit starts at level 4 or higher.
Promotion material, not a recruit pull.
The roster is empty. Recruitment has misplaced the applicants.
Someone found practical meaning in the report margins.
Lv. %1$d: +1 recovery slot from loot specialist
Lv. %1$d: +1 recovery slot from veteran handling
Breach, wall, obstacle, and simple quests gain a big score bonus.
Magic and ancient quests gain score, with a safer minimum roll.
Stealth and heist quests gain score and bonus loot on success.
Escort, guard, siege, and camp quests reduce risk.
Paperwork and contract quests gain score and extra gold.
Trap and exploration quests gain score and reduce risk.
Magic, ancient, and heist quests get a safer roll and bonus loot on success.
Heist and stealth quests gain score, gold, and bonus loot on success.
Wilderness, hunt, and exploration quests gain a strong score bonus.
Curse, poison, undead, holy, and rot quests reduce risk and block the worst failure.
Stealth and heist quests gain score and reduce risk.
Undead, curse, and debt quests gain score and bonus XP.
Paperwork, contract, and debt quests gain score and extra gold.
Holy, curse, escort, and guard quests reduce risk and block the worst failure.
Paperwork, contract, and debt quests get a safer roll and extra gold.
Wilderness, rot, and swamp quests gain score and reduce risk.
Undead, curse, and debt quests gain score and block the worst failure.
Long, siege, and camp quests reduce risk and grant bonus XP.
Obstacle, wall, collapse, and breach quests gain a huge score bonus.
Swamp, poison, and rot quests gain score and bonus loot on success.
Simple, breach, wall, and obstacle quests gain score; paperwork gets risky.
The ledger stayed rude and empty.
The job was done. Sadly, on purpose.
We half-won, which still counts as a bruise.
The expedition failed. The mud took notes.
Even the map filed a complaint.
%1$s hit the problem until it left.
%1$s was seen near the missing part.
%1$s tracked the trail back to lunch.
%1$s held the line and the line thanked him.
%1$s balanced the cave until it owed us money.
%1$s pruned the disaster into something tidy.
%1$s negotiated with death and got a discount.
%1$s seasoned the expedition until it behaved.
%1$s improved the floor plan with explosives.
%1$s salted the trail, the wounds, and the paperwork.
%1$s solved the problem by misunderstanding it aggressively.
The Cave of Minor Regrets apologized by dropping loose change.
The forest rearranged itself, then denied everything.
The bandit forms were stamped with unnecessary menace.
The chapel bell rang once and leaked brine.
The moon pretended not to see the cargo.
The bread-crate barricade held, mostly out of spite.
The last locked door considered the paperwork and blinked first.
The crypt itemized every scream and added interest.
The checkpoint stamped the party twice and charged for the echo.
The caravan\'s ghost accepted the manifest, then haunted the duplicate.
The night patrol swore the same oath until the lanterns looked supervised.
The banquet served soup, subpoenas, and one extremely audited dessert.
The wedding vows were cross-examined and found emotionally binding.
The toll booth bubbled up, charged exact change, and sank again.
The crown found the receipt under a very official sandwich.
The sideways tower leaned into compliance and became mostly vertical.
%1$s\'s charge found the shortest route through the warning signs.
%1$s read the glyphs aloud until they corrected themselves.
%1$s found a hidden pocket in the mission plan.
%1$s stood where danger expected a door.
%1$s turned the paperwork into a timed sport.
%1$s cited the terrain manual and the trap looked embarrassed.
%1$s\'s luck tripped over a better outcome.
%1$s made the shadows pay a convenience fee.
%1$s found the trail hiding behind the obvious trail.
%1$s cleaned the curse until it lost confidence.
%1$s left no trace, except a very smug invoice.
%1$s asked the dead for leverage. They had notes.
%1$s audited the threat until it produced gold.
%1$s\'s oath blocked the worst idea in the room.
%1$s balanced the books and unbalanced the enemy.
%1$s pruned the battlefield into something passable.
%1$s negotiated the death fees down to bruises.
%1$s distributed snacks until morale became structural.
%1$s introduced Plan B and removed several Plan A problems.
%1$s preserved the evidence, the snacks, and most of the party.
%1$s misunderstood the problem so hard it became simpler.
%1$s misunderstood the forms in a way the forms did not enjoy.
Pick the bad idea before launch. The report will remember who signed it.
Special Contract ready: %1$d stored. This launch spends 1 for an XXL payoff; standard plans stay free.
No Special Contract stored (%1$d). Loot contracts from successful jobs, passive reports, or charter rewards.
XXL clause: boosted gold, boosted loot, and +%1$d contract found.
XXL clause: boosted gold and boosted loot. No extra contract found this time.
No clever clause, no heroic accounting, no obvious lawsuit.
Return faster and squeeze a little more gold out of the panic, at the cost of risk.
Reduce risk for important attempts, but make spectacular victories harder to justify.
Check every suspicious crate. The job gets harder, but success pays in items.
Take longer and weaponize paperwork for better gold on documented trouble.
Spend time closing the suspicious route before it becomes the main route.
Trust the map everyone hates. It is faster, stranger, and occasionally full of loot.
Fight bandit bureaucracy with better bureaucracy and a very sharp stamp.
Turn swamp water into a defensive asset, or at least a less personal insult.
Cut through darker alleys for quicker delivery and a higher chance of regrettable extras.
Stretch siege supplies into morale, discipline, and several formal biscuit audits.
Confront the final lock with paperwork so complete the hinges feel supervised.
Read the undead debt ledger aloud and charge a documentation fee.
Collect and re-collect signatures before your party even leaves the gate.
Make the caravan submit the same manifest seven times to avoid arguing with a checkpoint captain.
Read the Oath of the Night
Make every night patrol recite the same oath and keep the same route map.
Serve the inspectorate in order and make the paperwork look expensive.
Count every promise as a liability before the cake can testify.
Search below the booth for fees, receipts, and whatever is still jingling.
Turn royal embarrassment into a signed route through the archives.
Make the tower admit which direction is up before anyone signs the climb.
The report found actual causes. This has made accounting nervous.
The shortcut worked. Several stairs were treated as optional, and the guild is choosing not to ask.
The party saved time, then spent most of it explaining why the shortcut was on fire.
Rush the Job became Rush the Apology. The report arrived before some of the dignity.
Safety First paid off: fewer bruises, cleaner receipts, and only moderate disappointment from the reckless members.
The safe route worked eventually. The party avoided danger, then found a smaller danger with better handwriting.
Safety First prevented the worst ideas, which sadly left the second-worst ideas in charge.
Loot Priority was justified. The party checked every crate and one crate checked back with valuables.
The loot detour found something useful, plus a reminder that crates can hold consequences.
Loot Priority became Crate Regret. The party learned that not every container wants a career change.
Audit Everything turned paperwork into leverage. The target surrendered after appendix C.
The audit uncovered value, confusion, and a receipt with hostile intent.
Audit Everything produced a complete file on why the expedition went wrong. Beautiful margins.
The side tunnel stayed sealed. The cave sulked, but the party returned with all exits accounted for.
The tunnel seal held until someone leaned on it for morale. Still, most of the cave behaved.
The side tunnel objected to being side content and became the expedition.
The terrible map led somewhere useful by accident. Cartography has asked for privacy.
The worst map was technically a route. The party found loot, daylight, and several wrong turns.
The map won the argument and lost the forest. Nobody is letting it hold a pen again.
Demand Receipts worked. The bandits surrendered after realizing your stamp had jurisdiction.
The receipts helped, although one bandit filed a counter-complaint shaped like a chair.
The paperwork was flawless. The bandits were not emotionally ready for compliance.
Bless the Brine turned the swamp into an ally with questionable table manners.
The brine helped. It also insisted on being mentioned in the minutes.
The swamp accepted the blessing, then returned it damp and annotated.
The moonless route paid off. No witnesses, several valuables, and only one suspicious shadow invoice.
The shortcut delivered the cargo and a fresh list of things not to describe in court.
The dark alley had its own plan, and it brought more friends.
Ration the Biscuits held the line. Morale rose after everyone received exactly one heroic crumb.
The biscuits lasted. The complaints lasted longer, but the barricade stayed up.
The ration plan failed when someone promoted crumbs to emergency currency.
The door forms were accepted. The lock opened out of professional embarrassment.
The forms confused the door long enough for progress, but not long enough for dignity.
The door rejected the forms for being too emotionally complete.
Itemized Last Rites settled the debt and billed the afterlife for administrative strain.
The ledger balanced, mostly. A few ghosts requested receipts and emotional damages.
The dead disputed the invoice and summoned a collections department.
